affordances.name: Incumbent option inertia test
affordances.mechanism: When staying the course sounds prudent, force the answer to test whether the incumbent option remains superior on evidence or is surviving because loss aversion, switching friction, default effects, or precedent make change feel costly.

use_when: A current default, routine, vendor, strategy, or plan is being favored over alternatives.
The team needs to separate real switching costs from inertia, sunk costs, or unchallenged precedent.
A proposal to stay the course sounds prudent but may be disguising loss aversion or status pressure.
do_not_use_when: The incumbent option is actually superior after evidence-backed comparison.
The proposed change is novelty seeking without baseline evidence that the new option is better.
The answer would treat every legacy choice as bad simply because it is old.
case_evidence_needed: The incumbent option and the alternative being considered.
The evidence that the status quo still performs better or worse.
The real switching costs, risks, and reversibility constraints.
The assumptions or social costs that make challenge to the default difficult.

treatment_requirements.description: Require the answer to compare the current default against a live alternative, separating real switching cost from inertia before recommending staying or changing.
evidence_required: The current default or incumbent path.
Evidence for relative performance, cost, risk, and reversibility.
The loss aversion, precedent, or social cost that may protect the default.
good_output_shape: States what evidence would justify staying.
States what evidence would justify changing.

diagnostic_questions: Is this option surviving because it is better, or because changing feels costly?
What real switching cost exists, and what part is just inertia or precedent?
What evidence would make staying the course the correct choice rather than the comfortable choice?
misuse_guards: Do not treat every legacy choice as bad just because it is old.
Do not turn anti-status-quo energy into novelty-seeking change without baseline evidence.
Do not ignore real switching costs while accusing the team of inertia.

source_evidence.source_quote: inherent preference for the current state or default option
test whether an incumbent option is surviving because it is actually superior or merely because changing feels costly
separate real switching costs from inertia
a proposal to "stay the course" sounds prudent but may be disguising loss aversion, sunk costs, or unchallenged precedent

review_notes.normalization_note: Batch 5 outcome: strong_affordance_record. The source supports an incumbent-option inertia test that is balanced: challenge defaults without assuming change is always better.
dropped_material: Did not merge choice-architecture examples into this card because diagnosing incumbent survival and designing a beneficial default require different receiver actions.
Did not extract general System 1 cognitive economy as a separate card.
open_questions: Future packet review should test overlap with commitment-bias, lock-in, path-dependence, and default-choice architecture.

affordances.name: Default choice architecture with opt-out guard
affordances.mechanism: When the task is to design a repeated or low-stakes choice environment, deliberately set beneficial defaults or reduce choice overload while preserving an easy opt-out and checking for exploitation, so inertia supports the user's goal instead of silently protecting the incumbent.

use_when: The task is designing enrollment, opt-in or opt-out paths, defaults, or choice sets rather than diagnosing whether an incumbent plan should survive.
Decisions are repeatable, low-impact, or made under time pressure, and a helpful default could reduce cognitive load.
Choice overload is causing inaction, no-choice behavior, or default sticking, and reducing the option set would improve the decision.
do_not_use_when: The default would exploit inertia for the designer's benefit against user welfare, informed consent, or easy reversal.
The case needs evidence-backed comparison of an incumbent and alternative rather than default or choice-set design.
The choice is high-stakes or irreversible enough that simplification would hide material trade-offs users must understand.
case_evidence_needed: The desired behavior, welfare outcome, or operating standard the default should support.
The current default, opt-in or opt-out path, and evidence of choice overload, inaction, or avoidable cognitive load.
The options to reduce, preserve, disclose, or make easy to reverse.
The risk that the default manipulates rather than helps the decision-maker.

treatment_requirements.description: Set the default around the user's welfare or operating goal, then preserve disclosure, opt-out, and reversal so status-quo inertia helps rather than traps.
evidence_required: The welfare, participation, decision-quality, or operating outcome the default is meant to support.
The default state and the active opt-in or opt-out behavior required today.
The opt-out, reversal, disclosure, or review path that keeps the default from becoming coercive.
Evidence that the default helps a repeatable or low-stakes choice rather than hides a material trade-off.
good_output_shape: Names the default and why it is beneficial in this context.
States the opt-out or reversal path plainly.
Separates helpful cognitive-load reduction from exploitative inertia.
Rejects default design when user welfare or informed reversal is not protected.

treatment_requirements.description: When too many options are causing paralysis, reduce the offered set while retaining the choices needed for meaningful user goals and decision quality.
evidence_required: The current option set and evidence that overload is producing inaction or default sticking.
The options that can be removed, bundled, sequenced, or deferred without harming the user's goal.
The high-stakes or preference-sensitive options that must remain visible.
The success measure showing the reduced set improves decisions rather than only conversion.
good_output_shape: Explains which choices are removed or sequenced and why.
Keeps materially different or high-stakes options visible.
Defines a decision-quality or welfare measure, not only an adoption metric.
Warns when simplification would become manipulation or information hiding.

diagnostic_questions: What beneficial default would reduce cognitive load without trapping the user?
What opt-out, reversal, or disclosure keeps this default from becoming exploitative?
Which choices can be reduced because they create paralysis, and which choices must stay visible because they change welfare or risk?
Are we designing a helpful default, or using inertia to avoid evidence-backed comparison?
misuse_guards: Do not use default design as a dark pattern that exploits inertia against the decision-maker's welfare.
Do not reduce choices by hiding materially different, high-stakes, or preference-sensitive options.
Do not use choice architecture to avoid comparing the incumbent against a live alternative when that is the actual decision.
Do not treat adoption caused by friction or opt-out difficulty as proof that the default is beneficial.

source_evidence.source_quote: In the real world, the Status Quo Bias is applied—or exploited—most commonly in situations involving decision architecture and organizational inertia.
If a company automatically enrolls employees in a 401(k) plan and requires them to actively opt-out, participation rates are significantly higher than if they must actively opt-in.
source_evidence.section_hint: Concrete Examples in Business and Strategy

source_evidence.source_quote: This uses the psychological inertia of the SQB to ensure the beneficial default option is maintained.
source_evidence.section_hint: Concrete Examples in Business and Strategy

source_evidence.source_quote: To overcome this, the **Less Frame** should be applied, which involves reducing the number of choices offered.
When decisions are repeatable, low-impact, or occur under time pressure, relying on the status quo or heuristics (fast, established thinking) is highly effective for reducing **cognitive load**.
source_evidence.section_hint: Contexts Where Status Quo Tendencies Are Most Powerful (Strengths)

review_notes.normalization_note: Split from the incumbent-option inertia test because this affordance designs defaults and option sets, while the existing card audits whether staying with an incumbent is evidence-backed or inertia-protected.
dropped_material: Did not split 401(k) defaulting and Less Frame into separate affordances; both are choice-architecture treatments that use inertia or choice reduction deliberately.
Did not promote general consistency, trust, or AI-persona style persistence into this affordance because those are not default or choice-set design transactions.
open_questions: Packet stress should verify that the exploitation guard remains visible when the card is rendered compactly.

absence_records.reason: The source explicitly warns against treating legacy choices as bad merely because they are old. The model requires evidence-backed comparison.

source_evidence.source_quote: every legacy choice is treated as bad simply because it is old

absence_records.reason: The source rejects change for novelty alone. It warns that breaking inertia without baseline evidence can become unsafe novelty seeking.

source_evidence.source_quote: novelty-seeking change without enough baseline evidence that the new option is actually better
The fundamental essence of the Status Quo Bias (SQB) is the **inherent preference for the current state or default option**.
The bias becomes dangerous when it is maintained blindly, leading to poor outcomes

review_notes.normalization_note: Batch 5 source reading found strong reviewed depth for Status Quo Bias. Extracted one incumbent-option comparison record and preserved absences for anti-legacy and novelty-seeking misuse.
dropped_material: The 401(k), Less Frame, and jam-study material is now represented in the default-choice-architecture affordance rather than collapsed into the incumbent-option comparison record.
Executive vision examples remain application context for incumbent-option inertia rather than separate records.
No runtime, prompt, lane, packet, or user-facing change is implied.
open_questions: Later packet comparison should check whether this card is distinct enough from commitment-bias and lock-in.
